Windows "running scripts is disabled on this system" — How to Fix PowerShell Execution Policy
Developers run shell commands constantly, and sometimes Windows PowerShell throws "running scripts is disabled on this system, so cannot load...". Writing down the fix.
"Running scripts is disabled on this system"

. : File C:\Users\Hsiangfeng\Documents\WindowsPowerShell\Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1 cannot be loaded because running scripts is disabled on this system. For more information, see about_Execution_Policies at https:/go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkID=135170.
At line:1 char:3
+ . 'C:\Users\Hsiangfeng\Documents\WindowsPowerShell\Microsoft.PowerShe ...
+   ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    + CategoryInfo          : SecurityError: (:) [], PSSecurityException
    + FullyQualifiedErrorId : UnauthorizedAccess
This happens because Windows has a security policy that prevents running arbitrary scripts (e.g., something malicious you didn't realize you were executing). Default
This typically shows up in Windows 10 PowerShell. Microsoft didn't publish exactly when the default was changed to Restricted, but for developers it's annoying — you'll want to switch it.
Quick rundown of the policy values (paraphrased from Microsoft's docs):
Requires all scripts and config files to be signed by a trusted publisher, including ones you wrote on the local machine.
Prompts you before running scripts from publishers you haven't classified as trusted or untrusted.
Risk — signed but malicious scripts can still run.
Nothing is blocked, no warnings or prompts.
Designed for configurations where PowerShell is part of a bigger app with its own security model.
Sets the default for your platform.
Requires a digital signature from a trusted publisher for scripts downloaded from the internet (including email/IM).
Doesn't require signatures on locally-written scripts.
Runs unsigned downloaded scripts if they've been unblocked (e.g., via
Risk — unsigned scripts from non-internet sources can still run; signed scripts can still be malicious.
— local scripts run freely; remote-downloaded scripts need a trusted publisher.
Permits individual commands, but no scripts.
— even signed/trusted scripts are blocked. Total lockout.
No execution policy is set in the current scope.
If undefined across all scopes, effective policy is Restricted.
Default for non-Windows machines, can't be changed.
Warns before running scripts from outside the local intranet.
Before changing anything, check the current state:
language-cmd shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
To fix the "running scripts is disabled" error:
, otherwise you'll get a permission error:
Run as Administrator — right-click the terminal → "Run as administrator":
Run the command again. You'll be prompted to confirm:

Execution Policy Change
The execution policy helps protect you from scripts that you don't trust. Changing the execution policy might expose you to the security risks described in the about_Execution_Policies help topic at
https:/go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkID=135170. Do you want to change the execution policy?
[Y] Yes  [A] Yes to All  [N] No  [L] No to All  [S] Suspend  [?] Help (default is "N"):
— which allows local scripts to run but still requires signed remote-downloaded scripts — security stays reasonable.
Close and reopen your terminal so the new policy takes effect properly.

Hit "running scripts is disabled on this system, so cannot load..." in Windows PowerShell? Windows defaults the Execution Policy to Restricted (especially on Windows 10 PowerShell), which blocks script execution as a security measure. This post explains the Execution Policy levels (AllSigned, Bypass, Default, RemoteSigned, Restricted, Undefined, Unrestricted) and how to switch to RemoteSigned so npm, vue, gulp, webpack, etc. work normally.
